speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 24, 1995. Survivor, Charlotte Melkman. The interviewer, Vanessa Ring. City, Sydney. Country, Australia. And the language, English. My name is Vanessa Ring, R-I-N-G. I'm interviewing Mrs. Charlotte Melkman. And it's the 24th of March, 1995, in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so if we could begin with your name and how to spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my name is Charlotte Melkman, M-E-L-K-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blokjesman, B-L-O-K-J-E-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and are you known by any other names, or were you at any stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's either Charlotte or Lottie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 13th of April, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so that makes you how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearly 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paisley, in Scotland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, how do you spell Paisley?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-I-S-L-E-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right. We'll begin the actual interview now. And we'll talk about your time in, in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You grew up in Holland, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from about my third year, although I can't remember that, of course. My parents used to live in Scotland, as you understand. And when I was about three years, they went back to Holland, to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1923.

speaker: interviewee
text: About that time, I would say, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. So we'll talk about growing up in Holland. And we'll start first with your family. Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what ages were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was 16 when he was taken away. And the other one was about two years younger than I, who was with me. And I think again, we'll talk to that-- about that later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were they both younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any other people live in the house with you apart from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Could you tell me a bit about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's not-- they used to live in Belgium. They only used to come over on holidays. And we used to go on holidays to stay with her. They came back to live in Holland, and my grandfather died. And she came to live with us. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a person was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lovely, lovely woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe her for me? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: She was tiny, reddish hair. You know, lovely like any grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so did she treat you very well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would she give you any kind of special treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how close were you to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too close, good-- good understanding. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and was it a house that you lived in, or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you describe the house for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] How would I say? My father had business at the house. So it was always-- customers used to come and used to come through the living room to-- I suppose-- he was an agency for British houses, and rain coats, and rubber boots and so on. So there was always activity in our house, and especially on rainy days, you know, he was very busy. And the living room was-- on the big table, they-- they used to make parcels, and trucks used to come to deliver and pick up. You know, so it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --everything in the house, kind of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was involved with the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, was it a large house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- he would import goods from England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then sell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he got it on consignment. And once a year, he used to go to England and Scotland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, he also had houses in Canada, for [INAUDIBLE] boots, rubber boots. He used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: The British-- Netherlands British Agency, NBA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And would your mother help out with that at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she used to do the typing, and when father wasn't home, she used to attend to the customers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was also quite involved in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I must tell you this. There was one customer who used to come. And during the war, at the beginning of the war, my father used to sell sails cloth to him. And not until a couple of years ago, I found out that that man who bought the sails cloth, was actually rebuilding fishing trollies into sea-faring boats who were going to-- trying to get over to England during the war. So whether my father knew it or not, he was involved a little bit in underground work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I only found it out a couple of years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very long story.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was with a friend in Holland. And he got a, a newspaper from the underground called 1940-45. And he said, hey Lottie, he said, did your father sell sails cloth? That was somebody writing the paper, that he was looking for people who used to-- he was trying to find out how many people reached England in those little boats. And then the name of a Mr. [? Birens ?] was mentioned. I said, I know Mr. [? Birens ?]. He used to come to our house. He was a customer. And so it proved that my father sold sails cloth to him, which he used to make little fishing boats sea-worthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but your father may or may not have been aware of what it was for.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether he know it and kept silent from myself. I, I don't know. I'll never know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. If we could talk about how religious your family was now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you practicing Jews? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we knew we were Jewish. We had a bit of Jewish education. We knew all about it, traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- What kind of education did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Sunday, we had special lessons. My father got a teacher in to, to give us lessons at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and did you have any religious meals, or did you follow the calendar at all? The Jewish calendar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew when the holidays were, like Passover, you know. We had the, the Jewish Pass-- you know, matzah. And-- but not really. Friday night was a little bit extra. Mother used to do extra cooking. Two-- she had two stoves going in the kitchen on a Friday. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of foods would you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, chicken, chicken soup, vegetables, you know. A little bit extra than during the week. And lots of customers, you know, I knew that when they came on a Friday, there was always something for them. [LAUGHS] My mother always served them a plate of soup or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of financial situation were you're living in when you were growing up? Was it a hard time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal, not rich, not poor, very normal. When it was a very rainy season, my father made, you know, money. And sometimes he-- you know, not regular income.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it really just depended on, on the weather.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the weather, yeah, more or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when it rained, was that something, you thought, oh, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rainy season was good, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of-- would you get treats and that sort of thing when things went better for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we-- whatever we needed, we got, you know. We never went short of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. And did your brothers have bar mitzvahs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you remember them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not very well, because they didn't make such a big business out of it as they do these days. These days, they get cars and overseas trips, and so on. I remember when-- [LAUGHS] in those days, boys went-- when they became bar mitzvahed, they got a first watch. That was a bar mitzvah present. Now, they're born with watches. It's different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the actual events, though, and what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really, no. Very vague.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. And so how much involvement did you have in the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing really. Not like here, that, you know, you volunteer for this, or volunteer for that. No, we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how important was being Jewish to you in those pre-war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, we just lived better, because there were so many Jews living in-- in Amsterdam, especially, that we never gave it a thought, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't feel that you were part of a strong community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just accepted that, and that was it. We were Jewish, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what about contact with non-Jewish people. How assimilated were you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. As I said, you know, customers came. Even at nighttime they used to come, and come in the house, have a cup of tea, you know, sit at the table. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you ever experience any antisemitism at all from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK. And is it possible to-- to say which you identified more strongly as being, whether it was being Jewish or being Dutch?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never gave it a thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't exist, you know? It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel a loyalty to the Dutch Queen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt-- you felt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when she came to visit Amsterdam, you know, we used to go out and stand in the street and wave. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Talking about the Queen, I read the other day, that the Crown Prince of Holland is probably marrying an Israeli girl. So that's the royal family in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's support from them for the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very much. Yeah. Yeah, very much they were for the Jews, the House of Orange, this, the royal family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what types of schools did you attend? Were they assimilated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me about the friends that you had there? Were they Jewish or non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: It really just was an issue at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what kinds of things did you enjoy doing in your younger years? What would you do out of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. [LAUGHS] Doing my homework.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about leisure activities, or-- or what you'd do after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go for a while to table tennis. And afterwards when we were not allowed to go anywhere, they had special Jewish clubs. And used to go to tennis on Saturday afternoon. That was already when the Germans were there. And also used to go to a, a sewing evening to learn to sew, also from the Jewish authorities, because that was all we were allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. We might talk about that a little bit later on. But in the meantime, what about neighbors? And were you close to any synagogues or anything like that in the area that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at the corner of our street, there was a Russian synagogue. And right next to us, as next door neighbors in the backyard, there was a little synagogue, Portuguese. Of course, there were lots of Portuguese, Spanish Portuguese people in Amsterdam. And when they used to go to that synagogue, they had to go through the front door of those people, through the long corridor, part of the kitchen, and so to the-- to the synagogue in the backyard-- and-- just over the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have any contact with the synagogue at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I used to go-- I loved to go there on a Thursday and polished all the copper. I didn't have to. But, you know, I liked to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just something that you volunteered to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, used to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you anything for doing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things would you be polishing? What were the artifacts that were in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, there's always lots of copper in a synagogue like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what, what kinds of objects where they? Like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Candles, candelabras-- ornaments. No, not ornaments, but decorative.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when did you leave school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was 15, after-- leaving examination. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '35 or-- '35, I think, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a assistant cashier in a big store, something like compared to Woolworth's. And I worked as an assistant cashier, and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the firm?

speaker: interviewee
text: HEMA.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you enjoy working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Loved it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it so good?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Because I had-- there were lots and lots of sales girls. And every department had a cash register. And I-- at night time, you know, I had to take the-- see how much everyone made. And in the morning, the girls used to come. And I used to have to count money, and make up the lists, and send to the headquarters. And, you know, I was a lot involved. I liked this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so who did you mix with at this time after you started working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a special girlfriend. We used to go out on a Saturday night, Sunday afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she was Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things would you do on Saturday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd just go to town, walk main streets, have a cup of coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe pre-war Holland for me? What were the main things that stand out in your mind from that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beautiful, beautiful country, free. Although we didn't-- we just took it for granted. It's a beautiful country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you see as you were strolling along in the evenings? Was that a big event that people always did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking at the windows, window shopping, you know, off of the main streets. I don't know. I can't remember really. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and over this time, were you at all aware of the Nazi rise that was going on in other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but we didn't pay much attention, you know. It's far away. You know, that's what-- although Holland did a lot in 1936. They took lots of German refugees in. And that's when they built the camp where we went later on, Westerbork. But not with barracks. They had houses. And, you know, people got houses, those German refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like when all these refugees came into Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had some friends. As a matter of fact, that man used to be a film photographer for the UFA in Germany. And he made lovely photos of us always. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the UFA?

speaker: interviewee
text: UFA, it's the German-- like MGM in, you know, different-- films, big German film studio at the time. And they were lovely people. We were-- became very friendly with them. They went to America later. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the German refugees get on with the Dutch Jews? Was there much conflict between them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other differences that you noticed after that came into the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young to pay any attention to that. That all went, you know, past me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what news did you actually hear, though, from what was going on outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I didn't pay much-- you know, as a young girl-- not like these days with all the television and so on. You don't pay much attention to it because it didn't, didn't concern you. You know? It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't feel any threat as a Jewish person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, did you notice any rise in antisemitic feeling over this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Holland. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and did you actually-- were you aware of any support from the Dutch population?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the occupation. Were you aware of, of the Dutch people sort of feeling sympathetic towards the Jewish plight outside Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't aware of anything that-- I'm probably not very helpful in that, because, you know, I was a young girl. You were not told much, and didn't think twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so what do you recall of the actual German occupation of Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, the first month, nothing. Everything went as normal. But then gradually, I had to register. We had to wear the star. We were not allowed in the trains, in the buses, not allowed in certain shops. There were shops only for Jews, and shops no Jews allowed. Not in parks, not travelling. You had to get a special pass if you wanted to travel. In by 8 o'clock at night, like a curfew. And, you know, it became worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you about the actual occupation? What happened when they actually came into the country? Do you remember seeing them coming in in their tanks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I woke up in the morning and grandmother-- the Germans have invaded. You have to be careful. Now we can't talk. And you have to be very careful and, and the bombardment of Rotterdam you heard on the radio. And it was very bad in Rotterdam. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel threatened as a Jewish person when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- me, not personal. Because, you know, as I said, I never paid much attention. But I think my parents did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that, that they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] May, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I then said to my father, look. Lots of people trying to get to England. Why don't we [INAUDIBLE]? It's not none of your business. Keep out of it. That's what the answer, you know. It was not up to the children. They were seen-- to be seen and not heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Father knows best, and he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any time did he change his mind, and think it might have been a good idea to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because there was not much opportunity. It was only a few days, and then, you know, after they invaded, and Holland capitulated, it was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you couldn't get out then anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and there was nothing we could flee to because, you know, the border was Germany. Belgium was also occupied. All we could do was [INAUDIBLE] to see, through the worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you actually had to wear the stars--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did the Dutch population do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had a big strike. Everybody stopped work. The trains-- the buses, the trains, everything, one day, and then just-- the Germans just picked up a lot of people, and shot them, and it was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was actually--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's still remembered every year as the February Strike. They've got a big statue in, in the city. And it's still remembered. See. They, they wanted to help, but they couldn't do much.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was February 1941, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And, you know, everybody says, oh, the Dutch were so good, you know. For every good one, there were two bad ones, or maybe the other way around. But there were a lot of bad ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And very, very many very good ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your experience of the bad ones at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that they would place-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were-- as soon as people were taken out of their houses to be sent to the camps, there used to come a big furniture van, Dutch people, and emptied the house, went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Dutch people actually take over the houses and end up living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was it like when that started happening for you? What was your reaction to all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad, very bad. Because that was the reason why my fiance was picked up. There was a big furniture van opposite this. And he came to say goodbye to me, because he went to a hiding place. And he didn't wear a star. And I wasn't home. And when they saw me coming in the street, he came up to me. I wore a star. He didn't, and those Dutch people from the furniture van picked him out. And that's how he was caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: For-- because they recognized him as being Jewish. So is it-- do you have resentment towards the Dutch people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long had you been going out with your fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long. I only met him in June, I think, and in September he went. But in the short time, we met for such a lot, it, it was like years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just in the street. Because I went out with some girlfriends, and wearing the star. And he was by himself. And he started talking to us with-- normally. You know, you don't answer. You don't go into that. But seeing he had a star, you know, it attracted those days. And he took us to an ice cream parlor. And then we took the-- my girlfriends home, and he took me home. And that's how it started. Normally, you know, I wouldn't pick up anyone from the street. I wouldn't have done that. But this time it was different, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything was very intense at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and as it had to be, I had neighbors. And his uncle, I'll tell you later about him, he was married to a lady who used to live next door. And after the first night when he-- when I met him, he went to them and made inquiries about me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was love at first sight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how soon after you'd actually met did he propose to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He never proposed. It all went automatically really. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of a Jewish ritual to it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we just went. And when I got my calling-up papers, and I thought I had to go. And we just bought a ring, and we were engaged. And that was it. And then I got a fortnight to wait before I went. And it became months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of my British-- I had British papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit more about that? How did people get called up? And where were they taken to? And what did you know about where people were being taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, when they were picked up from their homes, or when they had voluntary-- you know, when they got a call up and just had to go, we were going to a theatre, and assembled there. From there, the Germans worked out who had to go and who didn't have to go. And then they were sent to Westerbork. And from there, every Tuesday, the train would come. And in the middle of the night, the lights went on, and the lists were read out. And people whose name was called out started packing again. And the next morning after, went in the cattle trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were actually kept in this large theatre, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one day, two days, or sometimes a few hours. My parents apparently were in there only couple of hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you tell me about-- so you are called up. Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was picked-- picked up from home by the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE], how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just came in the house. And we just had to go. I said, yeah, but I'm British. You know, I don't have to go. They were Germans. And they said, oh, you know, work that out later. You have to come now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Bergen-Belsen. No, first to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if we can just talk about-- you were taken to the place where everybody was collected together. And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were taken to Westerbork straight away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what about your family? And-- were they taken as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were gone already. I was the last one in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if we could just talk about what happened then when your family was taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was taken. My parents were just-- there was a ring at the door. And there were the, the Dutch police picked them up. They just had to go. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any chance to say goodbye?

speaker: interviewee
text: And at-- we had very good friends. She was our housemate. And her husband tried everything the next day to try and do something for them. But it was too late. They had gone already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these friends were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were non-Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have a chance to say goodbye to them at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you-- to whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: To your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear from them after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But my fiance was picked up, like, a few days before them, before my parents. And he was taking to Briesen first. And they met up in the camp in Westerbork . And they went in the same train. He traveled with them until a certain town in Germany. And then the elderly had to get out. So my parents went out, and he stayed in. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last time that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I met his parents in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And your brothers, what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother, the youngest one, he was 16. He got-- the three of us got call-up papers. But my brother and myself got out of it for the time being because of British-- because we were born in Britain. And the young brother just went. He had to assemble at such-and-such a time, such-and-such a spot. And he just went. It will be all right. If I don't go, they'll take the parents. That's what everybody said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he felt that if he didn't-- if didn't go, then he would be putting other people in danger.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lots of people did not go. They went into hiding. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And was he called up by paper or mail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, the administration in Holland was so good, they had everything on paper. They're good. Not like in Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with Charlotte Melkman. It's the 24th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
